food_name: Smoky Beef Burger with Smoky Mayo
aliases: Smoky Beef Burger with Smoky Mayo

english: Smoky Beef Burger with Smoky Mayo

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for smoky beef burger with mayo sauce; closest defensible anchor is beef burger patty food product (FOODON_00002383).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Beef burger, cooked) is the closest defensible match; smoky mayo sauce component is not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT concept identified for smoky beef burger dish; null applied rather than risk fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard smoky beef burger serving is approximately 180 g, comprising a grilled beef patty with smoky mayo sauce and a bun.

nutrient_source.title: Smoky Beef — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Smoky Beef (cr_id G00010123) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported in the HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef burger patty, cooked (NDB 13357) as a supplementary reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for smoky beef burger with mayo. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous mixed beef-and-bread burger dish from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI ~45 for beef burger on white bun, low-GI estimate driven by high protein content slowing gastric emptying). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (7.5 g from 180 g portion × 4.19 g/100g) / 100 = 3.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 369 mg/100 g; borderline high despite strong potassium offset (368 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less and request reduced-salt marinade preparation, as sodium at 369 mg/100 g approaches the upper threshold for a single dish component; the high potassium content (368 mg/100 g) provides a partial offset but does not fully neutralise the sodium concern at standard serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (4.19 g/100 g), low sugar (1.45 g/100 g), high protein (18.48 g/100 g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content (4.19 g/100 g) and high protein (18.48 g/100 g) make this dish suitable for people with type 2 diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments; protein slows gastric emptying and attenuates post-meal glucose excursions, and the estimated glycaemic load per serving is low at 3.4.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.3 g/100 g), cholesterol (52 mg/100 g), sodium (369 mg/100 g), and trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from grilling
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation; the combination of saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, borderline sodium, and trace trans fat from grilling collectively elevates LDL-associated cardiovascular risk, and frequency should be limited to no more than once per week within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; trace trans fat from grilling is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat consumption to no more than once per week; the arachidonic acid content of beef promotes systemic inflammation, and trace trans fat from grilling further exacerbates inflammatory burden — substitution with oily fish rich in omega-3 fatty acids is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Smoky Beef is a Western-style beef burger dish popular in Singapore's fast-casual dining scene, consisting of a grilled beef patty served with a smoky mayonnaise sauce, typically assembled in a bun. The beef patty is seasoned and grilled to develop a characteristic smoky flavour profile, while the smoky mayo sauce adds a creamy, mildly tangy richness. Per 100 g, the dish provides 148 kcal, 18.48 g protein, 6.0 g fat, and only 4.19 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate option relative to most burger-style dishes. Sodium is 369 mg/100 g, placing it in the borderline range for sodium-sensitive individuals. The dish is suitable for people managing type 2 diabetes due to its low glycaemic load (estimated GL 3.4 per 180 g serving) and high protein content that moderates post-meal glucose response. It is not recommended for individuals with hypertension without sodium reduction, nor for those with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acid profile of red meat. Cardiovascular disease patients should consume it in moderation given the saturated fat, cholesterol, and trace trans fat content. The dish is halal-suitable in most Singapore preparations and is commonly consumed at lunch or dinner.
common_user_queries: Is smoky beef burger suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in a smoky beef burger?
Can I eat smoky beef with hypertension?
Is smoky beef burger high in protein?
What is the calorie count of smoky beef?
Is smoky beef burger good for weight loss?
What is the glycemic index of a beef burger?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010123, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef burger patty as a supplementary reference and documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, clearly documented.
notes: Saturated_fat_g (2.3), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.5), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.4), and trans_fat_g (0.1) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked beef burger patty (NDB 13357) as the HPB source does not report fatty acid breakdown; these values are approximate and should be replaced with direct measurement when available. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific smoky beef burger preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a direct match as of 2026-04-16. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 45) based on a mixed beef-and-bread burger from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in the approved GI source database. Halal status is assumed based on typical Singapore fast-casual beef burger preparation; kosher status is not confirmed. Allergen list (gluten, egg, soy) is indicative based on typical burger bun and mayo composition; consumers with allergies should verify with the specific vendor.
